description: ClawHQ backlog — derived from PRODUCT.md, ARCHITECTURE.md, README.md (2026-03-18)

items.title: Config schema + 14 landmine validator
items.intent: Establish the config contract that every config-generating command validates against. All 14 OpenClaw landmine rules must be enforced at the schema level so no silent misconfiguration ships to users.
items.description: FOUNDATION: Every config-generating command in ClawHQ tests against this schema. Without it, config generation has no contract and silent landmines ship to users. The 14 landmine validator is the difference between 'it works' and 'it works correctly'. Acceptance: all 14 rules validate correctly against OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md; config loader handles precedence merging (project > user > defaults).
steps: Define TypeScript types: OpenClawConfig, ClawHQConfig, DeploymentBundle, CronJobDefinition
Implement all 14 landmine validation rules from OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md
Build YAML config loader with precedence merging (project > user > defaults)
Add clear, actionable error messages for each validation failure
acceptanceCriteria: All 14 landmine rules validate correctly against OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md
Config loader merges project > user > defaults precedence correctly
TypeScript types exported for all config shapes
Invalid configs rejected with actionable error messages

items.title: Gateway WebSocket RPC client
items.intent: Give ClawHQ the ability to talk to a running OpenClaw agent. Every live operation depends on this RPC client — without it, ClawHQ can only write files, never observe or control a deployed agent.
items.description: FOUNDATION: Every live operation — deploy health checks, config patches, session queries — goes through this client. Without it, ClawHQ can only write files; it cannot talk to a running agent. Acceptance: RPC round-trip works with real Gateway; timeout + abort signal paths exercised; typed error classes surface correct user messages.
steps: Implement token-authenticated WebSocket client for OpenClaw Gateway
Add request/response RPC with typed errors and timeout handling
Implement AbortSignal support for cancellation
Create custom error classes: GatewayError, ConnectionError, AuthError, RateLimitError, RpcTimeoutError
Add health polling with configurable interval and timeout
acceptanceCriteria: RPC round-trip works with real Gateway
Timeout and abort signal paths exercised in tests
Typed error classes surface correct user messages
Health polling detects Gateway up/down state

items.title: Docker two-stage build + container hardening
items.intent: Deliver a two-stage Docker build that caches the base image and hardens every container to at least standard security posture by default. Users must never run an unhardened agent.
items.description: BUILD GATE: No Docker build = no running agent. The two-stage architecture matters for user experience: Stage 1 rebuilds rarely (base image), Stage 2 rebuilds fast (custom tools/skills). Hash-based change detection means 'clawhq build' doesn't waste the user's time. Four security postures mean users get hardened-by-default without knowing what cap_drop means. Acceptance: build produces a runnable image; Stage 1 cache hit on no-change; security posture applied without user configuration.
steps: Implement Stage 1 Dockerfile: base OpenClaw + apt packages (cached layer)
Implement Stage 2 Dockerfile: custom tools + skills (fast rebuild layer)
Add hash-based change detection for Stage 1 cache invalidation
Generate build manifest with image hash, layers, size
Implement four security postures: minimal, standard, hardened, paranoid
Apply cap_drop ALL, read-only rootfs, non-root UID 1000, ICC disabled, resource limits per posture
acceptanceCriteria: Build produces a runnable container image
Stage 1 cache hit when base image unchanged
Standard security posture applied by default without user configuration
Build manifest written with image hash and layer info

items.title: Deploy orchestration + preflight checks
items.intent: Make 'clawhq up' produce a running, reachable agent or a clear, actionable error — never silent failure. Every preflight check must block deploy if it fails.
items.description: USER MOMENT: This is what 'clawhq up' does — the user's first deploy experience. The full sequence (preflight -> compose -> firewall -> health -> smoke test) ensures the user gets a working agent or a clear error, never silent failure. Step-by-step progress reporting means the user isn't staring at a frozen terminal. Acceptance: 'clawhq up' produces a running agent; all 6 preflight checks surface actionable errors; health verify confirms agent is reachable before returning.
steps: Implement full deploy sequence: preflight -> compose up -> firewall apply -> health verify -> smoke test
Implement 6 preflight checks: Docker, images, config, secrets, ports, Ollama
Add graceful shutdown and restart with firewall reapply
Add AbortSignal support throughout the deploy pipeline
Implement step-by-step progress reporting via callback
acceptanceCriteria: 'clawhq up' produces a running agent or a clear, actionable error
All 6 preflight checks surface actionable errors on failure
Health verify confirms agent is reachable before returning success
Step-by-step progress visible in terminal during deploy

items.title: Egress firewall (iptables CLAWHQ_FWD chain)
items.intent: Enforce that no egress traffic leaves the agent container except to explicitly allowlisted domains. The privacy promise is architectural, not advisory.
items.description: PRIVACY PROMISE: The egress firewall is what makes 'zero data leaves your machine by default' true, not marketing. Without it, an agent tool or skill could call out to arbitrary domains. Per-integration domain allowlist means the user's email agent can reach email APIs and nothing else. Air-gap mode is the paranoid user's kill switch. Acceptance: docker bridge traffic blocked to non-allowlisted domains; DNS and HTTPS to allowlist work; firewall reapplied after compose down; 'clawhq doctor' verifies rules are active.
steps: Create dedicated iptables chain CLAWHQ_FWD: ESTABLISHED/RELATED -> DNS -> HTTPS allowlist -> LOG + DROP
Derive per-integration domain allowlist from config
Implement air-gap mode (block all egress)
Make apply/remove idempotent
Add verification with expected vs actual rule diff
Implement auto-reapply after docker compose down
acceptanceCriteria: Docker bridge traffic blocked to non-allowlisted domains
DNS and HTTPS to allowlisted domains work correctly
Firewall reapplied automatically after compose down
'clawhq doctor' verifies firewall rules are active
items.groomingNotes: SECOND GROOMING (updated) — failed once (reason: implementation failed, no further detail). BUG-009 (yaml parser fix) is ALREADY DONE — do not re-do it. IMPLEMENTATION CHECKLIST: (1) Create src/secure/firewall/firewall.ts — move applyFirewall()/removeFirewall() from src/build/launcher/firewall.ts, add verifyFirewall() that diffs expected vs actual iptables rules. (2) Add air-gap mode flag: skip allowlist, drop all outbound except ESTABLISHED/RELATED. (3) Detect Docker bridge interface dynamically via 'docker network inspect' — never hardcode. (4) Compose-down watcher for auto-reapply. (5) EXEC_DRY env var for test dry-run mode — iptables calls must be mockable. (6) Doctor integration: add verifyFirewall() check to existing src/operate/doctor/ check array. (7) sudo/privilege handling: detect non-root and surface actionable error. SUDO NOTE: iptables requires root — document requirement in error messages. TESTING: All tests must use EXEC_DRY=1 or a mock exec to avoid requiring root in CI. ACCEPTANCE CHECK: applyFirewall() idempotent (run twice = same rules); verifyFirewall() returns diff when rules are missing; air-gap mode drops all outbound; doctor check reports fail with fix message when rules absent. POST-COMPLETION BATCH: After CQ-005 sprint completes successfully, immediately mark CQ-041, BUG-020, and BUG-023 as readyForSprint — they are all blocked exclusively on this item.

items.title: PII + secrets scanner
items.intent: Surface any API keys, passwords, or PII in workspace files before deploy. False positives must not train users to ignore scan results.
items.description: USER VALUE: Security hardening, not blocking adoption. Scanning existing workspace for PII is important but the agent runs without it. Promote back to must when install flow is complete and security pass begins.
steps: Implement pattern-based scanner for API keys, passwords, tokens, PII
Add false-positive filtering for comments, placeholders, example.com
Implement redacted output display
Add recursive directory walk
Add git history scanning for committed secrets
acceptanceCriteria: Scanner detects common API key, password, and token patterns
False positives filtered for comments and placeholder values
Output shows redacted matches, not raw secret values
Git history scanning catches previously committed secrets

items.title: Secrets management (.env store)
items.intent: Ensure every credential written to disk is atomic, 0600-permissioned, and survives process interruption. No partial writes, no world-readable secrets.
items.description: SECURITY BASELINE: If .env is misconfigured or readable by other processes, the user's API keys are exposed. Atomic writes (temp + rename) and 0600 permissions are non-negotiable. This is the lowest layer of the credentials stack — everything CQ-008 (health probes) does depends on reliable .env reads. Acceptance: written files are always 0600; atomic write survives process kill mid-write; get/set/remove preserve format and comments.
steps: Implement .env file parser/reader/writer with format preservation
Add atomic writes via temp file + rename with 0600 permissions
Implement get/set/remove value operations
acceptanceCriteria: Written .env files are always 0600 permissions
Atomic write survives process kill mid-write (no partial files)
Get/set/remove preserve existing format and comments

items.title: Credential health probes
items.intent: Detect expired or invalid credentials before they cause silent agent failures. Every configured integration must report pass/fail with an actionable fix message.
items.description: SILENT FAILURE PREVENTION: Credential expiry is one of the top reasons agents stop working without explanation. A user who set up their Anthropic key three months ago has no idea it expired. Probes run on schedule and surface the problem before the next tool call silently fails. Built-in probes for Anthropic, OpenAI, Telegram cover the most common integrations. Acceptance: probe reports correct pass/fail for each integration; failed credential surfaces actionable message; 'clawhq creds' outputs clean table.
steps: Implement probe-based credential validation framework
Build probes for Anthropic, OpenAI, Telegram integrations
Define extensible probe interface for adding new integrations
Implement report aggregation for 'clawhq creds' output
acceptanceCriteria: Probes report correct pass/fail for each configured integration
Failed credentials surface actionable fix message
'clawhq creds' outputs clean status table
Probe interface extensible for new integrations

items.title: Blueprint loader + validator
items.intent: Load and validate any blueprint YAML — built-in or community — rejecting malformed input with clear errors. This is the read path the entire blueprint engine depends on.
items.description: BLUEPRINT ENGINE FOUNDATION: The loader is the reading end of the blueprint system. Without it, 'clawhq blueprint list/preview' (FEAT-001) and the init wizard (CQ-010) have nothing to work with. 70+ validation checks mean a malformed community blueprint can't corrupt the user's setup. Acceptance: all built-in blueprints load cleanly; invalid YAML surfaces readable error; size limit prevents DoS on large files; templateToChoice() works correctly for wizard display.
steps: Implement YAML blueprint loader from configs/blueprints/
Add 70+ validation checks for blueprint correctness
Enforce security baseline requirements in blueprints
Support multiple load paths (local, built-in)
Implement templateToChoice() conversion for wizard display
acceptanceCriteria: All built-in blueprints load without errors
Invalid YAML surfaces readable error message
Size limit prevents DoS on oversized blueprint files
templateToChoice() produces correct wizard display data

items.title: Init wizard + config generation + writer
items.intent: Guide a non-technical user from blueprint selection to a valid, landmine-free config in one interactive session. Generated config must pass all 14 validation rules.
items.description: CORE USER FLOW: This is the forging ceremony — the moment where a blueprint becomes a personalized agent. The guided wizard is the primary path for the Privacy Migrant persona who chose ClawHQ specifically because they don't want to hand-write 13,500 tokens of config. Air-gapped mode support ensures the wizard works without calling home. Acceptance: wizard completes without errors on happy path; generated config passes all 14 landmine rules; atomic writer never produces partial files; air-gap mode skips cloud-dependent steps gracefully.
steps: Build interactive setup wizard with guided prompts
Implement config generation enforcing all 14 landmine rules
Add atomic file writer (temp + rename)
Support air-gapped mode (skip cloud-dependent steps gracefully)
acceptanceCriteria: Wizard completes without errors on happy path
Generated config passes all 14 landmine validation rules
Atomic writer never produces partial config files
Air-gap mode skips cloud-dependent steps gracefully

items.title: Doctor diagnostics + auto-fix
items.intent: Make 'clawhq doctor' the single command that catches every known failure mode before it hits the user. Auto-fix must resolve common issues without requiring knowledge of internals.
items.description: HERO FEATURE: 'doctor' is the safety net that turns OpenClaw from 'it silently broke' to 'I know exactly what's wrong and how to fix it'. The 14+ checks cover every known failure mode preventively. Auto-fix means the user doesn't need to know what cap_drop is — they just run 'clawhq doctor --fix' and it works. Table output is the key UX: all checks visible at a glance, pass/fail clear, fix suggestions actionable. Acceptance: all 14+ checks run; auto-fix resolves common issues; table alignment clean; JSON output available for scripting.
steps: Implement 14+ preventive diagnostic checks covering all known failure modes
Add auto-fix capability for common issues
Build table output with pass/fail status per check
Add JSON output mode for scripting
acceptanceCriteria: All 14+ diagnostic checks run and report status
Auto-fix resolves common issues (e.g., cap_drop, permissions)
Table output aligned and readable at a glance
JSON output available for scripting and automation

items.title: Encrypted backup + restore
items.intent: Deliver encrypted, integrity-verified snapshots that restore a working agent state. Post-restore doctor check must confirm the restored agent is healthy.
items.description: USER VALUE: Day-2 operational maturity. Encrypted backup is essential for production use but does not block first working agent. Users need a running agent before they need to snapshot it. Promote to must before first public release.
steps: Implement GPG-encrypted snapshot creation
Add SHA-256 integrity verification on restore
Use temp directory isolation during restore operations
Run post-restore doctor check automatically
acceptanceCriteria: Snapshots are GPG-encrypted at rest
SHA-256 integrity verified before any restore
Post-restore doctor check confirms healthy agent state
Restore from backup produces a fully working agent

items.title: CLI entry point + UX helpers
items.intent: Ship the CLI entry point so every command is reachable, errors are human-readable, and first-run detects missing prerequisites. Nothing runs without this.
items.description: HIGH VALUE: Gate item — nothing runs without this. Commander.js flat CLI (AD-01), first-run detection, spinner/error UX helpers. Acceptance: clawhq --help lists all commands; error handler formats cleanly; first-run detects missing install.
steps: Set up Commander.js flat CLI with all command registrations
Implement error handler with formatted terminal output
Add first-run detection for missing prerequisites
Build spinner and formatError UX helpers
acceptanceCriteria: 'clawhq --help' lists all registered commands
Error handler formats errors cleanly for terminal display
First-run detects missing install and guides user to 'clawhq install'

items.title: clawhq install — pre-req detection + scaffold
items.intent: Make 'clawhq install' the first command that works — detect prerequisites, scaffold ~/.clawhq/, and leave the user with clear next-step guidance.
items.description: HIGH VALUE: This is the literal first command a user runs. Gate: Docker detected, Node >=20, Ollama running. Creates ~/.clawhq/ scaffold. Without this, nothing else works. Acceptance: 'clawhq install' exits 0, ~/.clawhq/ structure exists, clawhq.yaml written, user sees next-step guidance.
steps: Detect Docker, Node.js >=20, Ollama prerequisites
Create ~/.clawhq/ directory with correct structure
Write initial clawhq.yaml with sensible defaults
Scaffold all required subdirectories
Display clear next-step guidance on success
acceptanceCriteria: 'clawhq install' exits 0 on success
~/.clawhq/ structure exists with correct layout
clawhq.yaml written with defaults
User sees clear next-step guidance after install

items.title: clawhq install --from-source
items.intent: Enable zero-trust users to clone, audit, and build OpenClaw from source with the same install command. The from-source path must produce an identical engine artifact.
items.description: USER VALUE: Advanced zero-trust acquisition for security-paranoid users. Not on default install path. Blocked by CQ-014 and CQ-003 anyway. Build after trusted-cache install is working end-to-end.
steps: Clone OpenClaw repository from source
Run two-stage Docker build from cloned source
Store built engine artifact in ~/.clawhq/engine/
Verify built artifact matches release artifact
acceptanceCriteria: From-source build produces identical engine artifact to cached install
Build completes without network calls beyond initial clone
Engine artifact stored in ~/.clawhq/engine/
items.groomingNotes: All dependencies done (CQ-014 install scaffold, CQ-003 two-stage Docker build). Zero-trust acquisition path for security-paranoid users. Clones OpenClaw, runs two-stage build from source, stores artifact to ~/.clawhq/engine/. Artifact verification step ensures parity with trusted cache.

items.title: ~/.clawhq/ deployment directory migration
items.intent: Replace all hardcoded ~/.openclaw/ paths with configurable ~/.clawhq/ deployment root. Existing installations must migrate without data loss.
items.description: USER VALUE: Internal path migration — replaces hardcoded ~/.openclaw/ with configurable ~/.clawhq/. User-invisible refactor. Does not block MVP. Do after install scaffold (CQ-014) is working.
steps: Make deployment root configurable via clawhq.yaml
Replace all hardcoded ~/.openclaw/ paths with configurable root
Implement migration command for existing installations
acceptanceCriteria: Configurable deployment root respected throughout codebase
Migration command moves existing data without loss
Existing ~/.openclaw/ installations migrate cleanly to ~/.clawhq/
items.groomingNotes: REVISED SCOPE: grep shows 6 files with .openclaw/ references, but ALL are container-internal paths (/home/node/.openclaw/) — where OpenClaw looks for its config inside the Docker container. These are correct per AD-03 (tight coupling to OpenClaw) and must NOT be changed. Host deployment paths are already parameterized via deployDir (from clawhq.yaml). ACTUAL WORK: (1) audit that every host-path reference in ClawHQ CLI uses the configurable deploymentRoot, not a hardcoded ~/.openclaw/ or ~/.clawhq/; (2) if any host-paths are hardcoded, replace with config lookup; (3) add migration command that moves ~/.openclaw/ to ~/.clawhq/ for existing pre-release installs. Estimated scope: small — host paths already appear to use deployDir. Verify first before sprint.

items.title: Email Manager blueprint YAML
items.intent: Deliver the Email Manager blueprint as the reference implementation — email triage, calendar, tasks, cron, hardened security. This is the demo-ready first use case.
items.description: HIGH VALUE: THE first use case — this is what we show in demos. Defines Email Manager end-to-end: email + calendar + tasks tools, email-digest + morning-brief skills, 15min inbox cron, 8am daily digest. Acceptance: clawhq blueprint preview email-manager shows complete design; forge from CQ-010 produces valid openclaw.json. Must pair with CQ-022 (email-digest skill).
steps: Define Email Manager blueprint YAML with email + calendar + tasks tools
Configure email-digest and morning-brief skills in blueprint
Set up 15min inbox cron and 8am daily digest schedule
Apply hardened security posture with minimal egress allowlist
acceptanceCriteria: 'clawhq blueprint preview email-manager' shows complete design
Forge via init wizard produces valid openclaw.json
Blueprint defines all tools, skills, cron schedules, and security settings
Blueprint pairs correctly with email-digest skill (CQ-022)

items.title: Blueprint personalizer
items.intent: Blueprint wizard must ask 1-3 personalization questions per blueprint so forged agents reflect user-specific preferences, not generic defaults.
items.description: USER VALUE: Phase 2 personalization. Adds customization_questions to blueprint schema so wizard asks 1-3 tailored questions. Valuable but not blocking: Email Manager (CQ-017) must work end-to-end first. Build after CQ-010 + CQ-017 prove the forge pipeline.
steps: Add customization_questions field to blueprint YAML schema
Implement wizard prompts for 1-3 questions per blueprint
Apply user answers to config generation output
acceptanceCriteria: Blueprint YAML supports customization_questions field
Wizard asks 1-3 tailored questions per blueprint
Answers reflected in generated config output
items.groomingNotes: All dependencies done (CQ-010 init wizard, CQ-017 email-manager blueprint). Phase 2 personalization: add customization_questions field to blueprint YAML schema, have wizard ask 1-3 tailored questions per blueprint, apply answers to config output. Additive change to existing wizard flow — no rework required.

items.title: Identity file generators (SOUL.md, AGENTS.md, etc.)
items.intent: Generate SOUL.md and AGENTS.md from the blueprint so every forged agent has a distinct identity. Identity files must be read-only at runtime — agents cannot rewrite who they are.
items.description: IDENTITY IS THE PRODUCT: SOUL.md defines who the agent IS — its personality, communication style, boundaries. Without it, the user gets a generic OpenClaw instance, not a purpose-built agent. Read-only mount means the agent cannot modify its own personality at runtime (LM-12 prevention). SOUL.md (not IDENTITY.md) per current docs. Acceptance: SOUL.md generated from blueprint + wizard answers; AGENTS.md reflects correct tool/skill inventory; identity files mounted read-only in container; token budget respected.
steps: Generate SOUL.md from blueprint definition + wizard answers
Generate AGENTS.md reflecting correct tool/skill inventory
Mount identity files read-only in container
Enforce token budget for identity content
acceptanceCriteria: SOUL.md generated with personality, communication style, boundaries from blueprint
AGENTS.md reflects correct tool/skill inventory
Identity files mounted read-only in container (LM-12 prevention)
Token budget respected — identity content fits within limits

items.title: Workspace tool generators
items.intent: Generate all 7 workspace tool wrappers from blueprint config so the agent can act on the world. An agent without its tools is decoration.
items.description: AGENT'S HANDS: Tools are what the agent can DO. An email agent without an email tool can't triage inbox. Blueprint-driven generation means tools are consistent with what the identity and skills expect. CLI wrappers (bash/python3) are thin — they call himalaya, caldav, etc. — so adding a new integration means writing a wrapper, not modifying the agent. Acceptance: all 7 tool wrappers generate correctly from blueprint config; generated tools executable in container; tool names match blueprint's tool references in AGENTS.md.
steps: Generate CLI tool wrappers for: email, tasks, todoist, ical, quote, tavily, todoist-sync
Derive tool configuration from blueprint YAML
Ensure generated wrappers are executable in container
Match tool names to blueprint's tool references in AGENTS.md
acceptanceCriteria: All 7 tool wrappers generate correctly from blueprint config
Generated tools are executable in container
Tool names match blueprint's tool references in AGENTS.md

items.title: Skill lifecycle + vetting
items.intent: Every skill installation must pass security vetting before activation. Rollback must restore the previous state if a skill breaks the agent.
items.description: CAPABILITY GATE: Skill lifecycle is what makes skills safe. Without it, installing a skill means manually copying files with no vetting, no rollback, no audit. The email-digest skill (CQ-022) must go through this pipeline. The URL trap fix is critical — malicious skills should not be able to call out to arbitrary hosts. Rollback snapshots mean 'clawhq skill install' is reversible. Acceptance: install flow runs stage->vet->approve->activate; vet catches URL patterns; rollback restores previous state; 'clawhq skill list' shows installed skills with status.
steps: Implement stage -> vet -> approve -> activate pipeline
Add rollback snapshots before each installation
Implement security vetting with URL trap detection
Build 'clawhq skill list' showing installed skills with status
acceptanceCriteria: Install flow runs full stage -> vet -> approve -> activate pipeline
Vetting catches malicious URL patterns in skill code
Rollback restores previous state on failure or rejection
'clawhq skill list' shows installed skills with status

items.title: Skill: email-digest
items.intent: Deliver the email-digest skill so the Email Manager agent triages inbox, categorizes, and proposes responses with approval gates. No auto-send, no data leaves the machine.
items.description: HIGH VALUE: First autonomous capability the user sees. Pairs with CQ-017 (Email Manager blueprint). This skill is why the user chose ClawHQ over Gmail. SEQUENCING: Requires CQ-021 (skill lifecycle + vetting — email-digest must go through stage->vet->approve->activate pipeline), CQ-025 (approval queue — 'propose responses with approval gates' is the core safety feature), and CQ-017 (Email Manager blueprint to define context). Acceptance: SKILL.md + prompts installed via skill lifecycle pipeline; agent triages inbox, categorizes emails, proposes responses with approval gates; runs on 15min cron; no data leaves machine (local Ollama).
steps: Create SKILL.md defining email triage behavior and boundaries
Write prompts for inbox categorization, summarization, and response proposal
Configure 15min cron schedule for inbox checks
Integrate with approval queue — proposed responses require user approval
acceptanceCriteria: SKILL.md + prompts installed via skill lifecycle pipeline
Agent triages inbox and categorizes emails correctly
Proposed responses queue for approval (no auto-send)
Runs on 15min cron schedule
No data leaves machine (local Ollama only)

items.title: Audit trail (tool + egress + secrets)
items.intent: Log every tool execution, secret access, and egress event to tamper-evident JSONL. The user must be able to answer 'what did my agent do?' at any time.
items.description: TRANSPARENCY PROMISE: 'You know what your agent did, what data it touched, and what left your machine' — this is a design principle. Without audit trail, it's just a claim. The user should be able to ask 'what did my agent do today?' and get an answer. HMAC-chaining on secret lifecycle means the audit is tamper-evident, not just logged. OWASP compliance export matters for the Tinkerer persona running in an org context. Acceptance: every tool execution logged to JSONL; secret lifecycle events HMAC-chained; 'clawhq audit' shows recent activity; export produces OWASP-compatible format.
steps: Implement tool execution audit logging to append-only JSONL
Add egress event logging
Implement HMAC-chained secret lifecycle events
Build OWASP compliance export format
Add 'clawhq audit' command for recent activity view
acceptanceCriteria: Every tool execution logged to append-only JSONL
Secret lifecycle events HMAC-chained (tamper-evident)
'clawhq audit' shows recent activity
Export produces OWASP-compatible format

items.title: Export + destroy (data sovereignty)
items.intent: Users must be able to export a portable agent bundle and cryptographically prove destruction. Zero lock-in — data sovereignty is the exit guarantee.
items.description: EXIT GUARANTEE: Privacy Migrant won't commit to a sovereign agent without knowing they can leave. 'clawhq export gives you everything portable. clawhq destroy proves it's gone. Zero lock-in.' — this is the product promise. PII masking on export means they can share the export without leaking data. Cryptographic proof of destruction is what makes 'destroy' trustworthy vs. just deleting files. Acceptance: export produces self-contained portable bundle; PII masked in export; destroy wipes all local data; crypto proof can be independently verified.
steps: Implement portable export bundling all agent data
Add PII masking to export output
Implement verified destruction with cryptographic proof
Ensure export bundle is self-contained and portable
acceptanceCriteria: Export produces self-contained portable bundle
PII masked in exported data
Destroy wipes all local data completely
Crypto proof of destruction independently verifiable

items.title: Approval queue
items.intent: High-stakes agent actions must queue for user approval before execution. No auto-send, no auto-buy, no auto-delete without explicit user consent.
items.description: SAFE AUTOMATION: Without approval queue, the agent can't safely propose actions that have real-world consequences. The email-digest skill must propose draft replies for approval — not auto-send. Telegram integration means the user gets notified on their phone, not just in a terminal. Resolution tracking means the agent knows what was approved vs. rejected, and can learn from the pattern. Acceptance: high-stakes actions queue for approval; Telegram notification sent; user can approve/reject from Telegram; resolution recorded in audit trail.
steps: Implement runtime approval queue for high-stakes actions
Add Telegram integration for approval notifications
Build approve/reject flow accessible from Telegram
Record resolution (approved/rejected) in audit trail
acceptanceCriteria: High-stakes actions queue for approval before execution
Telegram notification sent for each pending approval
User can approve/reject directly from Telegram
Resolution recorded in audit trail

items.title: Status dashboard + logs + updates
items.intent: Deliver status, logs, and update commands so operators can observe agent state, diagnose failures, and safely update without data loss.
items.description: USER VALUE: Important day-2 ops visibility but not blocking first agent. Users need a working agent before they need to watch its status. Depends on CQ-003+CQ-012 anyway. Promote to must when ops track begins.
steps: Implement 'clawhq status [--watch]' showing current agent state
Implement 'clawhq logs' for viewing agent logs
Implement 'clawhq update [--check]' with automatic rollback on failure
acceptanceCriteria: 'clawhq status' shows current agent state clearly
'clawhq status --watch' updates in real-time
'clawhq logs' displays agent logs
'clawhq update' applies updates with rollback on failure

items.title: Messaging channel connection
items.intent: clawhq connect must configure and verify a messaging channel so the user can talk to their agent. Without this, the agent exists but is unreachable.
items.description: HIGH VALUE: The last step before the user has a working agent. Without messaging channel, the agent exists but the user can't talk to it. SEQUENCING: Must run after CQ-002 (gateway client — health ping requires WebSocket RPC) and CQ-004 (agent must be deployed before channel can be connected). Acceptance: 'clawhq connect' guides Telegram/WhatsApp bot token setup + verifies with live health ping via Gateway WebSocket; user receives a test message from their new agent.
steps: Implement 'clawhq connect' guided setup for Telegram bot token
Add WhatsApp channel setup support
Verify connection with live health ping via Gateway WebSocket
Send test message to confirm agent is reachable
acceptanceCriteria: 'clawhq connect' guides Telegram/WhatsApp bot token setup
Health ping verifies connection via Gateway WebSocket
User receives a test message from their agent on completion

items.title: Quickstart (init -> build -> deploy -> verify)
items.intent: clawhq quickstart must take a user from zero to a running, reachable agent in one command. Any failure must leave clear guidance, not a half-deployed mess.
items.description: HIGH VALUE: The target user experience — one command to working agent. Phase-labeled spinners: Install -> Init -> Build -> Deploy -> Connect -> Verify. Missing dependency: also requires CQ-014 (install scaffold). Not ready until CQ-010+CQ-003+CQ-004+CQ-014 all land. Acceptance: 'clawhq quickstart --blueprint email-manager' produces a running, reachable agent with Telegram connected.
steps: Implement 'clawhq quickstart [--blueprint] [--smart]' orchestrator command
Chain phases: install -> init -> build -> deploy -> connect -> verify
Add phase-labeled spinners showing progress for each step
Reuse existing command logic — no new implementation logic
acceptanceCriteria: 'clawhq quickstart --blueprint email-manager' produces a running, reachable agent
Phase-labeled spinners show progress for each step
Failure at any step leaves clear next-step guidance
Telegram connected and verified at completion
items.groomingNotes: All direct dependencies done (CQ-010, CQ-003, CQ-004, CQ-014). SEQUENCING NOTE: Sprint CQ-005 (egress firewall) before this — CQ-004 deploy sequence includes 'firewall apply' step, so quickstart will silently skip firewall until CQ-005 is implemented. Quickstart without firewall is incomplete for the privacy promise. Pure orchestration command — no new implementation logic, chains existing commands. Phase spinners are the only net-new UX work. Run CQ-005 first, then this.

items.title: Additional blueprint YAMLs (6)
items.intent: Ship 6 additional blueprint YAMLs that validate against the schema and render correctly in blueprint list/preview. Every blueprint must be forgeable.
items.description: BLUEPRINT LIBRARY: 6 additional blueprint YAMLs — Family Hub, Founder's Ops, Replace Google Assistant, Replace ChatGPT Plus, Replace my PA, Research Co-pilot. Validate against current blueprint schema (CQ-009), adapt naming/paths, add missing fields (email-manager-adjacent skills, cron). Email Manager (CQ-017) is separate and higher priority — do not include it here. Acceptance: 'clawhq blueprint list' shows 7+ blueprints; 'clawhq blueprint preview <each>' renders valid design.

Create Research Co-pilot blueprint YAML
Validate all against blueprint schema from CQ-009

'clawhq blueprint preview <name>' renders valid design for each
All blueprints pass schema validation
Every blueprint is forgeable via init wizard

items.title: Additional skills (5)
items.intent: Ship 5 skills that install cleanly, run on cron, and make their respective blueprints actually useful. A blueprint without skills is an empty promise.
items.description: BLUEPRINT COMPLETENESS: Skills are the value-add that make each blueprint useful. morning-brief is what makes 'Replace Google Assistant' actually replace Google Assistant. market-scan is why Stock Trading blueprint earns its name. Each skill is a standalone SKILL.md + prompts — thin to implement, high user impact. Acceptance: each skill installs via 'clawhq skill install'; SKILL.md + prompts present; skill runs on configured cron schedule.
steps: Create morning-brief skill (SKILL.md + prompts)
Create market-scan skill (SKILL.md + prompts)
Create meal-plan skill (SKILL.md + prompts)
Create investor-update skill (SKILL.md + prompts)
Create schedule-guard skill (SKILL.md + prompts)
Verify each installs via skill lifecycle pipeline
acceptanceCriteria: Each skill installs via 'clawhq skill install'
SKILL.md + prompts present for each skill
Each skill runs on its configured cron schedule

items.title: Tool installation framework
items.intent: clawhq tool install must add tools and trigger a Stage 2 rebuild automatically. Users must never hand-edit Dockerfiles to extend agent capabilities.
items.description: EXTENSIBILITY: Tool install framework is how users extend their agent's capabilities after initial setup. Without it, adding a new tool requires manual Dockerfile edits. Dockerfile patching + Stage 2 rebuild trigger means 'clawhq tool install' just works. Depends on CQ-003 (two-stage build) being complete. Acceptance: 'clawhq tool install <name>' adds tool to Dockerfile, triggers Stage 2 rebuild; 'clawhq tool list' shows installed tools; 'clawhq tool remove' cleanly uninstalls.
steps: Implement 'clawhq tool install <name>' with Dockerfile patching
Implement 'clawhq tool list' showing installed tools
Implement 'clawhq tool remove' for clean uninstall
Trigger Stage 2 rebuild automatically on tool changes
acceptanceCriteria: 'clawhq tool install <name>' adds tool to Dockerfile and triggers Stage 2 rebuild
'clawhq tool list' shows installed tools
'clawhq tool remove' cleanly uninstalls and triggers rebuild

items.title: Notifications + alerts + self-repair + digest
items.intent: Proactively alert users to agent health issues and auto-recover from common failures. Users should not discover their agent died by noticing silence.
items.description: DAY-2 OPS: Users who've had an agent for 30+ days need to know it's healthy without checking manually. Predictive health alerts catch problems before they become failures. Auto-recovery handles the common cases (container stopped, OOM) without user intervention. Daily digest gives the 'what did my agent do today?' answer proactively. Acceptance: health alerts fire before service goes down; auto-recovery handles restart + OOM cases; daily digest delivered via configured channel.
steps: Implement multi-channel notification system
Add predictive health alerts based on resource trends
Implement auto-recovery for container stopped and OOM cases
Build daily digest delivery via configured channel
acceptanceCriteria: Health alerts fire before service goes down
Auto-recovery handles container restart and OOM cases
Daily digest delivered via configured notification channel

items.title: AI config inference (--smart)
items.intent: clawhq init --smart must infer the right blueprint and integrations from natural language, fully local via Ollama. No data leaves the machine.
items.description: DELIGHT PATH: 'clawhq init --smart' is the < 5 minute path. Users describe what they need, Ollama infers blueprint + integrations. Local Ollama means zero data leaves. This is the product's wow moment. Correctly positioned as should — guided wizard (CQ-010) must work first as the fallback path. Acceptance: natural language description maps to correct blueprint; integration suggestions accurate; result produces same output as guided wizard; works fully locally with Ollama.
steps: Implement natural language to blueprint inference via local Ollama
Map user description to blueprint selection + integration suggestions
Ensure inference result matches guided wizard output format
acceptanceCriteria: Natural language description maps to correct blueprint
Integration suggestions are accurate for described use case
Result produces same output format as guided wizard
Works fully locally with Ollama — no data leaves machine

items.title: Memory lifecycle + autonomy + trace + learning
items.intent: Manage agent memory across hot/warm/cold tiers so the agent gets smarter over time without bloating context. PII must be masked before cold storage.
items.description: LONG-TERM RETENTION: Memory lifecycle is what makes the agent smarter over time. Hot/warm/cold tiers prevent the 360KB memory bloat problem described in PRODUCT.md. Preference learning is the '6 months' story: 'It knows your top clients are always priority.' Not blocking initial adoption but critical for the 30-day churn metric. Acceptance: hot/warm/cold tiers active; LLM-powered summarization runs on schedule; PII masked before cold tier; agent preference patterns visible to user.
steps: Implement hot/warm/cold memory tiers with lifecycle transitions
Add LLM-powered summarization on configurable schedule
Apply PII masking before cold tier storage
Build autonomy recommendations from preference patterns
Implement decision trace logging
acceptanceCriteria: Hot/warm/cold tiers active with automatic lifecycle transitions
LLM-powered summarization runs on schedule
PII masked before cold tier storage
Agent preference patterns visible to user

items.title: Migration import (ChatGPT + Google Assistant)
items.intent: Import ChatGPT and Google Assistant exports so migrating users start with preferences intact on day 1. All processing local, PII masked.
items.description: MIGRATION STORY: Lowers the barrier for Privacy Migrants coming from ChatGPT or Google Assistant. Extracting preferences from export data means the forged agent already knows the user's patterns on day 1. PII masking during import keeps sensitive data local. Cron mapping from routines is the key value-add — not just importing preferences but translating them to scheduled jobs. Acceptance: ChatGPT export parsed; preferences extracted via Ollama; routine → cron mapping produced; PII masked; import completes without network calls.
steps: Parse ChatGPT data export format
Parse Google Assistant data export format
Extract user preferences via local Ollama
Map exported routines to cron job definitions
Apply PII masking during import processing
acceptanceCriteria: ChatGPT export parsed successfully
Preferences extracted via local Ollama
Routine-to-cron mapping produced correctly
PII masked during import
Import completes without any network calls

items.title: Integration + provider management + roles
items.intent: Deliver integration, provider, and role management so users add services and govern access through commands, not config file edits.
items.description: INTEGRATION MANAGEMENT: As agents grow (Month 1-6 story), users add integrations. Integration management makes this clean vs. ad-hoc file editing. Provider abstraction means credential routing is automatic. Identity governance (roles) matters for Fleet Operator persona managing agents for multiple people. Acceptance: 'clawhq integrate' connects new service with live validation; 'clawhq provider' manages cloud API routing; role definitions control what each integration can access.
steps: Implement 'clawhq integrate' for connecting new services with live validation
Implement 'clawhq provider' for cloud API credential routing
Implement 'clawhq role' for identity governance and access control
acceptanceCriteria: 'clawhq integrate' connects service with live validation
'clawhq provider' manages cloud API routing correctly
Role definitions control what each integration can access

items.title: Evolve rollback + credentials.json + posture.yaml
items.intent: Separate credentials into credentials.json (mode 0600), expose security posture in posture.yaml, and ensure every capability change has a rollback snapshot.
items.description: SECURITY MATURITY: credentials.json separation from .env is the difference between 'works' and 'production-grade'. Integration credentials in a dedicated store (mode 0600) means rotate one integration without touching others. Security posture YAML makes the container hardening visible and reviewable — the Tinkerer persona wants to see what posture they're running. Rollback snapshots for all capability changes complete the safety story started in CQ-021. Acceptance: credentials.json separate from .env; posture.yaml reflects active security settings; rollback restores previous capability state.
steps: Separate credentials into credentials.json with mode 0600
Create posture.yaml reflecting active security settings
Implement rollback snapshots for all capability changes
acceptanceCriteria: credentials.json separate from .env with 0600 permissions
posture.yaml reflects active security settings and is human-reviewable
Rollback restores previous capability state cleanly

items.title: Trust modes + heartbeat + command queue
items.intent: Implement three trust modes so users choose their privacy level. Kill switch must disconnect immediately. Signed command queue must reject tampered commands.
items.description: MANAGED MODE FOUNDATION: Trust modes are what makes the cloud layer safe by design. Paranoid users run fully offline. Zero-Trust users get optional remote monitoring without any inbound access. Managed users get operational automation with content architecturally blocked. Kill switch ('clawhq cloud disconnect') with no confirmation prompt is the user's emergency brake. Signed command queue prevents malicious commands even if cloud is compromised. Acceptance: all three trust modes switch cleanly; kill switch disconnects immediately; command signature verified before execution; unsupported commands rejected in all modes.
steps: Implement paranoid, zero-trust, and managed trust modes
Add kill switch ('clawhq cloud disconnect') with no confirmation prompt
Build outbound health reporter (heartbeat)
Implement signed command queue with signature verification
acceptanceCriteria: All three trust modes switch cleanly
Kill switch disconnects immediately with no prompt
Command signatures verified before execution
Unsupported commands rejected in all modes

items.title: Fleet management
items.intent: Fleet management must discover all running agents and aggregate health into a single view. Fleet-wide doctor must surface issues across all agents in one check.
items.description: FLEET OPERATOR: Multi-agent discovery + fleet-wide doctor is for the Fleet Operator persona managing agents for multiple people. Single pane of glass: see which agents are healthy, which need attention. Fleet-wide doctor means one check to confirm all agents are configured correctly. Not blocking single-user adoption. Acceptance: 'clawhq cloud fleet' discovers running agents; fleet health shows aggregate status; fleet doctor surfaces issues across all agents.
steps: Implement multi-agent discovery across running instances
Build health aggregation into single fleet view
Add fleet-wide doctor command
acceptanceCriteria: 'clawhq cloud fleet' discovers all running agents
Fleet health shows aggregate status in single view
Fleet doctor surfaces issues across all agents in one check

items.title: Web dashboard (server + 7 pages)
items.intent: Web dashboard must expose full operational control — doctor, logs, approvals, init — not a read-only view. Browser users get the same capability as CLI users.
items.description: WEB UI OPTION: Hono + htmx + Pico CSS keeps the stack minimal. Web dashboard is for users who prefer a browser to a terminal for day-to-day ops. Not blocking CLI users. Depends on CQ-011 (doctor), CQ-026 (status), CQ-025 (approvals) being solid first — web is just a UI layer over CLI-complete features. Acceptance: all 7 pages render; doctor page shows same output as CLI; approvals queue shows pending items; init wizard works via browser.
steps: Set up Hono + htmx + Pico CSS server
Build Home page with agent overview
Build Doctor page matching CLI output
Build Logs page with log viewer
Build Deploy page with deploy controls
Build Approvals page showing pending queue
Build Skills page with install/remove
Build Init Wizard page for browser-based setup
acceptanceCriteria: All 7 pages render correctly
Doctor page shows same output as CLI
Approvals queue shows pending items with approve/reject
Init wizard completes full setup via browser
items.groomingNotes: All dependencies done (CQ-011 doctor, CQ-026 status/logs, CQ-025 approvals). Hono + htmx + Pico CSS stack. Web is a UI layer over CLI-complete features — no new business logic. BUG-006 (src/server/ path) should be done first but is not a hard blocker. 7 pages are well-scoped.

items.title: Air-gapped mode
items.intent: Air-gapped mode must block all egress at config and firewall layers. Doctor must verify both layers active. The agent must function without any network access.
items.description: PARANOID MODE: Air-gapped deployment for users who want zero network access from the agent. Config-level (zero egress in openclaw.json) + firewall-level (CLAWHQ_FWD blocks all outbound) hardening in combination. Doctor verifies both layers are active. Use case: sensitive environments, compliance requirements, maximum privacy. Depends on CQ-005 (firewall) and CQ-010 (wizard) being complete first. Acceptance: air-gap mode sets zero egress in config; firewall blocks all outbound; doctor confirms air-gap active; agent functional without any network access.
steps: Set zero egress in openclaw.json config for air-gap mode
Configure CLAWHQ_FWD firewall chain to block all outbound traffic
Add doctor check verifying both config and firewall layers active
acceptanceCriteria: Air-gap mode sets zero egress in config
Firewall blocks all outbound traffic
Doctor confirms air-gap is active at both layers
Agent remains fully functional without any network access
items.groomingNotes: THIRD GROOMING (failCount: 2) — Analysis shows firewall blocking, CLI flags, and doctor check are ALL ALREADY IMPLEMENTED. The sprint kept failing because of one specific missing piece: explicit zero-egress config in the generated openclaw.json. WHAT IS DONE: (1) applyFirewall() accepts airGap option and skips allowlist rules when true (src/build/launcher/firewall.ts:46-82); (2) --air-gap CLI flag on 'clawhq up' and 'clawhq restart' (src/cli/index.ts:1054, 1149); (3) doctor check 'air-gap-active' verifies empty allowlist + no ACCEPT rules for port 443/53 (src/operate/doctor/checks.ts:686-741); (4) ClawHQConfig.security.airGap field exists (src/config/types.ts:276). WHAT IS MISSING (the blocker): src/design/configure/generate.ts sets airGapped cloud mode to paranoid but does NOT write an explicit empty egress list to the generated openclaw.json config. When the sprint validates 'Air-gap mode sets zero egress in config' it finds no egress field set. THE FIX: In src/design/configure/generate.ts, locate where openclaw.json fields are written. When answers.airGapped === true, set the egress/allowedDomains field to [] (empty array) in the generated config output. Grep 'airGapped' in generate.ts to find the right location — around line 420-421. Verify the field name in the OpenClaw config schema (CQ-001 — src/config/types.ts OpenClawConfig type). ACCEPTANCE CHECK: run init wizard with air-gap option; inspect generated openclaw.json for an explicit egress domains field set to []; run 'clawhq doctor' and verify 'air-gap-active' check passes. All iptables calls must use EXEC_DRY=1 in tests.

items.title: clawhq blueprint list + preview commands
items.intent: clawhq blueprint list and preview must let users evaluate every available blueprint before committing to init. Users must never choose a blueprint blind.
items.description: HIGH VALUE: Gate for informed blueprint selection. Without clawhq blueprint list/preview, users run init blind — they can't evaluate options before committing. Pairs tightly with CQ-009 (loader already does the heavy lifting). Build immediately after CQ-009. Acceptance: 'clawhq blueprint list' shows all available blueprints with one-line descriptions; 'clawhq blueprint preview email-manager' renders full design (identity, tools, skills, cron, integrations, security posture, egress domains). No net-new logic — thin CLI surface over CQ-009.

Implement 'clawhq blueprint preview <name>' showing full blueprint design

'clawhq blueprint preview email-manager' renders full design
Preview shows identity, tools, skills, cron, integrations, security posture, egress domains

items.title: Cloud provisioning engine — spin up agents on DO/AWS/GCP from CLI
items.intent: One command creates a running agent on a cloud provider. The user never touches a VPS console, SSH, or cloud-init. ClawHQ handles everything from VM creation to agent deployment.
items.description: Core provisioning engine in src/cloud/provisioning/. Provider-agnostic interface with DigitalOcean as first implementation, AWS and GCP to follow. Flow: user runs `clawhq deploy --provider do` → ClawHQ creates VM via provider API → injects cloud-init (Docker + clawhq bootstrap) → waits for agent health → returns URL. Uses pre-built VM snapshots for speed where providers support them. Credentials stored locally in ~/.clawhq/cloud/credentials.json (mode 0600). VM metadata tracked in ~/.clawhq/cloud/instances.json for lifecycle management.
steps: Define provider interface: ProvisionOptions, ProvisionResult, ProviderAdapter
Implement DigitalOcean adapter: create/destroy/status via DO API v2
Build cloud-init template: Docker install, clawhq install, agent bootstrap
Add instance registry: track provisioned VMs in ~/.clawhq/cloud/instances.json
Implement health polling: wait for agent to report healthy after provision
Wire CLI: clawhq deploy --provider do/aws/gcp with interactive credential setup
acceptanceCriteria: clawhq deploy --provider do creates a running agent on DigitalOcean
VM is provisioned, Docker installed, agent deployed, health verified — all automated
Cloud credentials stored securely (mode 0600, never in config files)
Instance lifecycle tracked: create, status, destroy from CLI
Provider interface is generic enough for AWS/GCP without rewrite
items.groomingNotes: EPIC ANCHOR — all cloud provisioning items (CQ-044, CQ-045, CQ-046, CQ-047, CQ-048) depend on this. No blocking dependencies. SCOPE NOTE: CQ-043 includes a working DigitalOcean implementation end-to-end (step 2 overlaps with CQ-044 intentionally — CQ-043 is the reference implementation, CQ-044 adds production polish). This is a large sprint (~500-800 lines of new code) but all prior feature sprints of comparable scope (CQ-002, CQ-003, CQ-004) completed successfully. IMPLEMENTATION CHECKLIST: (1) Create src/cloud/provisioning/ directory structure: index.ts, types.ts, engine.ts, registry.ts, health.ts, providers/ subdir; (2) Define ProvisionOptions, ProvisionResult, ProviderAdapter interface in types.ts — must be extensible for AWS/GCP without rewrite; (3) Implement DigitalOceanAdapter in providers/digitalocean.ts using DO API v2 (POST /v2/droplets with user_data); (4) Instance registry: read/write ~/.clawhq/cloud/instances.json with atomic writes (temp+rename), 0600 permissions, try-catch on both read (JSON.parse) and write; (5) Health polling: poll agent /health endpoint after provision, configurable timeout; (6) Wire CLI: clawhq deploy --provider do with interactive API token setup — store token in ~/.clawhq/cloud/credentials.json (0600). CONSTANTS: instance registry path and cloud credentials path must use DEPLOY_CLOUD_SUBDIR constant (BUG-057 adds this). SECURITY: credentials stored 0600, never in config files per platform constraint. TESTING: mock DO API responses — do not require real DO API token in CI. POST-COMPLETION: mark CQ-044, CQ-047, CQ-048 readyForSprint and add BUG-058 (CLAUDE.md doc update) to ready state.

items.title: DigitalOcean provider adapter
items.intent: DigitalOcean is the fastest path to cloud deployment. Existing OpenClaw hosting on DO means the ecosystem is proven. First provider, reference implementation for the adapter interface.
items.description: Implement DigitalOceanAdapter in src/cloud/provisioning/providers/digitalocean.ts. Uses DO API v2 for droplet lifecycle. Supports both cloud-init (universal) and pre-built snapshot (fast) paths. Smallest viable droplet: s-1vcpu-1gb ($6/mo). Includes SSH key injection for emergency access, firewall group creation (ports 443 + gateway only), and floating IP for stable DNS. API token stored in ~/.clawhq/cloud/credentials.json.
steps: Implement DO API v2 client: droplets, SSH keys, firewalls, snapshots
Create droplet with cloud-init: Docker + clawhq + agent bootstrap script
Build pre-built snapshot workflow: create golden image, register as DO snapshot
Add DO firewall group: allow 443 (HTTPS) + gateway port, block everything else
Implement status polling: wait for droplet active + agent healthy
Add destroy with verification: terminate droplet, confirm deletion
acceptanceCriteria: DO droplet created and agent running within 3 minutes (cloud-init) or 60 seconds (snapshot)
Firewall restricts inbound to HTTPS + gateway port only
API token setup is interactive with validation before storage
Droplet can be destroyed cleanly from CLI
Cost is transparent: shown during provision and in status output
items.groomingNotes: Depends on CQ-043 (cloud provisioning engine) which must be complete first. CQ-043 includes a reference DO implementation — CQ-044 adds production polish: pre-built snapshot workflow, floating IP, comprehensive status output, cost transparency. NOT READY until CQ-043 is done. After CQ-043 completes, mark this ready. IMPLEMENTATION NOTES: DO API v2 client in src/cloud/provisioning/providers/digitalocean.ts (CQ-043 creates this file — CQ-044 extends it). Key additions over CQ-043's reference impl: (1) snapshot creation pipeline (provision base VM, install, snapshot, destroy builder); (2) floating IP assignment for stable DNS; (3) cost display in status output — DO API returns droplet hourly cost; (4) interactive API token validation before storage (test token via GET /v2/account). TESTING: mock DO API responses; test snapshot create/use paths separately. POST-CQ-043 UNLOCK: when CQ-043 merges, mark this readyForSprint.

items.title: AWS provider adapter (EC2 + cloud-init)
items.intent: AWS is where enterprise and power users already have accounts. Supporting EC2 removes the "I only use AWS" blocker.
items.description: Implement AWSAdapter in src/cloud/provisioning/providers/aws.ts. Uses AWS SDK v3 for EC2 lifecycle. Launches t3.micro (free tier eligible) or t3.small with cloud-init user data. Creates security group (443 + gateway port inbound), key pair for emergency SSH. Supports AMI snapshots for fast provision. Credentials via AWS CLI profile or explicit access key — stored in ~/.clawhq/cloud/credentials.json, never in environment.
steps: Implement EC2 client via AWS SDK v3: launch, terminate, describe, security groups
Create cloud-init user data script for Amazon Linux 2023 / Ubuntu
Build security group: inbound 443 + gateway, outbound all (agent firewall handles egress)
Add AMI snapshot workflow for fast re-provision
Implement region selection with latency estimation
Wire into provider interface with credential validation
acceptanceCriteria: EC2 instance launched and agent running within 3 minutes
Security group restricts inbound to HTTPS + gateway port
Works with existing AWS CLI credentials or explicit key entry
Free tier eligible instance type used by default
Instance can be stopped/started/terminated from CLI
items.groomingNotes: Second provider. AWS SDK v3 is TypeScript-native — good fit. Key complexity: IAM permissions. The user needs ec2:RunInstances, ec2:TerminateInstances, ec2:CreateSecurityGroup, etc. Document the minimum IAM policy. Consider: should we support AWS Lightsail as a simpler alternative to raw EC2?

items.title: GCP provider adapter (Compute Engine + cloud-init)
items.intent: GCP completes the big-three cloud coverage. Same pattern as AWS adapter.
items.description: Implement GCPAdapter in src/cloud/provisioning/providers/gcp.ts. Uses Google Cloud Compute API for VM lifecycle. Launches e2-micro (free tier) or e2-small. Creates firewall rule on default network. Credentials via gcloud CLI or service account JSON. Same cloud-init bootstrap as DO/AWS.
steps: Implement Compute Engine client: create, delete, get, set metadata
Create cloud-init startup script for Debian/Ubuntu GCE images
Build firewall rule: allow 443 + gateway port from 0.0.0.0/0
Implement machine image snapshot for fast re-provision
Wire into provider interface with credential validation
acceptanceCriteria: GCE instance launched and agent running within 3 minutes
Firewall rule restricts inbound to HTTPS + gateway port
Works with gcloud CLI credentials or service account key
Free tier eligible instance type used by default
items.groomingNotes: Third cloud provider. GCP complexity: project selection, zone vs region, firewall rules on VPC network. Consider deferring until DO + Hetzner + AWS cover the primary user base. The Privacy Migrant persona is covered by DO + Hetzner. Enterprise users are covered by AWS. GCP is a third provider for completeness. Sprint after CQ-045 (AWS) is complete and the pattern is proven across three providers. NOT READY until CQ-045 (AWS) is done — mark readyForSprint when CQ-045 completes.

items.title: clawhq deploy — unified cloud deployment command
items.intent: Single command to deploy an agent to any supported cloud provider. The user picks a provider, clawhq does the rest.
items.description: Wire the provisioning engine into a user-facing `clawhq deploy` command. Interactive flow: choose provider → enter/confirm credentials → select region → select blueprint → provision → deploy → verify → return URL. Non-interactive mode with flags for CI/automation. Shows cost estimate before confirming. Integrates with existing cloud module (trust modes, heartbeat) for post-deploy monitoring.
steps: Build interactive deploy wizard: provider → credentials → region → blueprint → confirm
Add cost estimation: show monthly cost before provisioning
Implement non-interactive mode: --provider, --region, --blueprint flags
Post-deploy: auto-configure trust mode (zero-trust default), start heartbeat
Add clawhq deploy status: show all cloud-deployed agents
Add clawhq deploy destroy: teardown with confirmation
acceptanceCriteria: clawhq deploy walks user through cloud deployment end-to-end
Cost shown before confirmation — no surprise bills
Non-interactive mode works for automation
Post-deploy monitoring configured automatically
Destroy is clean: VM terminated, DNS removed, local state cleaned
items.groomingNotes: Depends on CQ-043 (engine, DONE) and CQ-044 (DO adapter). This is the user-facing command that ties the entire cloud deployment story together — provider selection, cost estimate, provisioning, post-deploy monitoring. Must feel as simple as 'clawhq up' but for cloud. READYFORSPRING: mark ready when CQ-044 completes. Key UX requirement: the user never sees a cloud console — everything happens in their terminal. Interactive flow: choose provider → enter/confirm credentials → select region → select blueprint → show cost estimate → confirm → provision → deploy → verify → return URL. Non-interactive mode with --provider --region --blueprint --yes flags for CI/automation. Post-deploy: auto-configure zero-trust mode, start heartbeat. Destroy is clean: VM terminated, DNS removed, local state cleaned. CRITICAL: show monthly cost estimate BEFORE provisioning — no surprise bills.

items.title: Cloud-init bootstrap template
items.intent: A single cloud-init script that takes a bare VM to a running ClawHQ agent. Provider-agnostic — works on DO, AWS, GCP, Hetzner, any cloud with cloud-init support.
items.description: Template in src/cloud/provisioning/cloud-init.ts that generates a cloud-init user data script. Steps: install Docker, install clawhq, run clawhq install + init with provided blueprint config, clawhq up, signal health to provisioning engine. Parameterized: blueprint name, gateway token, trust mode, deploy dir. Must be idempotent (safe to re-run on reboot). Tested against Ubuntu 22.04 and Debian 12.
steps: Build cloud-init template generator with parameter injection
Docker install section: apt-get, Docker CE, enable on boot
ClawHQ install section: npm install -g clawhq or from source
Agent bootstrap: clawhq install → init with blueprint → up
Health signal: curl back to provisioning engine when agent is live
Make idempotent: skip steps that are already complete on re-run
acceptanceCriteria: Cloud-init takes a bare Ubuntu/Debian VM to running agent without manual steps
Parameterized: blueprint, tokens, trust mode all injected at provision time
Idempotent: safe to re-run on reboot without duplicating work
Health signal fires when agent is ready
Works on Ubuntu 22.04 and Debian 12
items.groomingNotes: Depends on CQ-043 (cloud provisioning engine) which must be complete first. NOT READY until CQ-043 is done. After CQ-043 completes, mark this ready alongside CQ-044. IMPLEMENTATION NOTES: Template in src/cloud/provisioning/cloud-init.ts. The template is a function generateCloudInit(params: CloudInitParams): string that produces a cloud-init YAML user-data script. Params: blueprintName, gatewayToken, trustMode, clawhqVersion, deployDir. Script sections: (1) #cloud-config header; (2) apt: install docker.io, curl, nodejs; (3) runcmd: enable docker, install clawhq via npm, run clawhq install, inject blueprint config from params, run clawhq up, curl health endpoint back to provisioning engine. IDEMPOTENT: use if/then guards around each install step. HEALTH SIGNAL: use a simple curl with retry to the provisioning engine's callback URL — include the droplet's public IP in the signal. TESTED AGAINST: Ubuntu 22.04 LTS and Debian 12 (these are standard DO, AWS, GCP base images). POST-CQ-043 UNLOCK: when CQ-043 merges, mark this readyForSprint.

items.title: Pre-built VM snapshots for sub-60s provisioning
items.intent: Cloud-init takes 2-3 minutes. Pre-built snapshots with Docker + ClawHQ pre-installed provision in under 60 seconds. This is what makes clawhq demo feel instant.
items.description: Snapshot builder in src/cloud/provisioning/snapshot.ts. Creates a golden VM image per provider: provisions base VM, installs Docker + clawhq, cleans up, snapshots. The snapshot is reused for all future provisions — only blueprint config and tokens are injected at boot time via metadata/user-data. Snapshot version tracked and auto-rebuilt when clawhq version changes.
steps: Build snapshot creation pipeline: provision → install → snapshot → destroy builder
Implement per-provider snapshot management (DO snapshots, AWS AMIs, GCP images)
Add boot-time config injection: blueprint + tokens via cloud metadata
Version tracking: rebuild snapshot when clawhq version changes
Add clawhq cloud snapshot build/list/delete commands
acceptanceCriteria: Snapshot-based provision produces running agent in under 60 seconds
Snapshot auto-rebuilt when clawhq version changes
Boot-time config injection works without re-running full install
Snapshot management commands available in CLI
items.groomingNotes: Optimization layer. Not required for MVP but required for the 60-second demo promise. DO snapshots cost $0.05/GB/mo — negligible. AWS AMIs are free to store. Build this after CQ-048 (cloud-init) proves the bootstrap flow works.

items.title: clawhq demo — zero-config working agent in 60 seconds
items.intent: Remove every gate between a curious person and a running agent. One command, no API keys, no Docker expertise, no messaging setup. The sovereignty story only matters to people who have experienced the product.
items.description: Ship a `clawhq demo` command that spins up a fully working agent with zero configuration. Bundled Ollama model (or fallback to mock LLM), mock messaging channel rendered in a localhost web UI, pre-loaded blueprint (replace-chatgpt-plus). The user types one command, waits ~60s, and is talking to their agent in a browser. No Telegram token, no Docker Compose knowledge, no .env files. This is the command that turns ClawHQ from an architecture into a product. Scope: (1) demo blueprint with sensible defaults and no external dependencies; (2) embedded web chat UI on localhost (extend CQ-040 dashboard with /chat route or standalone); (3) auto-pull smallest viable Ollama model if available, mock LLM fallback if not; (4) single-use ephemeral mode — demo data lives in a temp dir and is cleaned up on exit; (5) colorful terminal output showing what is happening at each step. Must work on a fresh machine with only Docker installed. PHASE 2 (post-CQ-044): add --cloud flag using DO provisioning for zero-Docker path.
items.groomingNotes: READY TO SPRINT — Tech Lead clearance per prior grooming. CQ-040 (web dashboard) is DONE, the only dependency. SCOPE: (1) configs/blueprints/demo.yaml — demo blueprint with zero external deps, no Telegram token, no email config, no API keys; (2) add /chat route to existing CQ-040 Hono server in src/web/ — browser chat UI for direct agent interaction via Gateway WebSocket; (3) Ollama auto-detection: use llama3:8b if available, else fallback to mock LLM returning 'I am an AI agent [model: mock]. You said: {message}'; (4) ephemeral mode: tempdir for deploy data, SIGINT cleanup handler, demo data never persists; (5) clawhq demo CLI command chains build (if needed) -> deploy (demo blueprint) -> open browser -> chat. ACCEPTANCE: clawhq demo on fresh Docker machine → user in browser chat within 60s, no prompts. PHASE 2 (post-CQ-044 only): --cloud flag for zero-Docker path. Do not implement phase 2 in this sprint.

items.title: Hetzner Cloud provider adapter (CX series + cloud-init)
items.intent: Hetzner Cloud adapter provisions a running agent on CX11. EU-sovereign path for the Privacy Migrant persona. Works identically to DigitalOcean adapter.
items.description: Implement HetznerAdapter in src/cloud/provisioning/providers/hetzner.ts. Uses Hetzner Cloud API v1 for server lifecycle. Launches CX11 (2.96 EUR/mo) or CX21. Creates firewall rules (443 + gateway port). Hetzner is GDPR-compliant, EU-hosted, privacy-conscious, and explicitly called out in CLAUDE.md as a target deployment platform alongside DigitalOcean. Same cloud-init bootstrap as DO/AWS/GCP adapters. Credentials via Hetzner API token stored in ~/.clawhq/cloud/credentials.json (mode 0600). Key differentiator for the Privacy Migrant persona: Hetzner is cheaper than AWS/GCP and explicitly European-sovereign.
items.groomingNotes: UPGRADED TO MUST — CLAUDE.md explicitly names Hetzner alongside DigitalOcean as a primary deployment target ('User's PC, Mac Mini, DigitalOcean, Hetzner — same software, different host'). Privacy Migrant is the primary persona; they prefer EU-sovereign, GDPR-compliant hosting at a lower price point. DO + Hetzner together cover the primary personas — AWS/GCP cover enterprise secondary users. CQ-043 (ProviderAdapter interface) is DONE. CQ-044 (DO adapter) should be done first as the reference implementation, but FEAT-003 can sprint in parallel since the interface is stable. IMPLEMENTATION: HetznerAdapter in src/cloud/provisioning/providers/hetzner.ts following exact same pattern as providers/digitalocean.ts. Hetzner Cloud API v1. Key endpoints: POST /servers (cx11, ubuntu-24.04, user_data=cloud-init), DELETE /servers/{id}, GET /servers/{id}. Add 'hetzner' to CloudProvider union type in types.ts. Data centers: FSN/NBG (Germany), HEL (Finland) — GDPR-compliant. TESTING: mock Hetzner API responses same as DO tests.

items.title: clawhq demo --cloud — zero-config cloud demo (phase 2)
items.intent: clawhq demo --cloud takes a user from zero to a cloud-hosted agent demo in 60 seconds with no Docker, no setup, just a DO API token. The droplet self-destructs on exit.
items.description: Phase 2 of FEAT-002 demo command. After CQ-044 (DO adapter) ships, add --cloud flag to 'clawhq demo' that provisions an ephemeral DigitalOcean droplet, spins up the demo agent in the cloud, opens the browser chat UI pointing at the cloud URL, and destroys the droplet on exit. Zero Docker requirement on the user's machine — they just need a DO API token. Scope: add --cloud flag to existing demo command; provision ephemeral DO droplet using CQ-043 engine + CQ-044 adapter; inject demo blueprint + mock LLM config via cloud-init (CQ-048); open browser pointing at cloud agent URL; cleanup droplet on SIGINT/exit. Cost: minimal — cx1 droplet for ~60s demo = fractions of a cent.
items.groomingNotes: Depends on FEAT-002 (base demo command, DONE when FEAT-002 ships), CQ-044 (DO adapter), and CQ-048 (cloud-init template). NOT READY until all three dependencies complete. Phase 2 of the demo story — zero Docker path for users who just want to try before installing locally. Ephemeral DO droplet: provision, run demo, destroy on exit. Blueprint: same demo.yaml as FEAT-002. LLM: inject mock LLM or Ollama config via cloud-init params. Browser: open same /chat UI but pointing at cloud URL. COST TRANSPARENCY: show estimated cost before provisioning (fractions of a cent for 60s). CLEANUP: SIGINT handler destroys droplet immediately — no orphaned instances.
